name: Amyloid beta-peptide (1-42)
relevance: Aggregation of Aβ42 into oligomers and fibrils is a central event in Alzheimer's disease. Understanding how this peptide folds and misfolds is directly relevant to the amyloid hypothesis and to structure-based drug design.
citation: Crescenzi O, Tomaselli S, Guerrini R, Salvadori S, D'Ursi AM, Temussi PA, Picone D. "Solution structure of the Alzheimer amyloid beta-peptide (1-42) in an apolar microenvironment." Eur J Biochem. 2002;269(22):5642-8.
source: RCSB PDB entry 1IYT (SEQRES + HELIX records, NMR solution structure)
note: The starting conformation is a randomized, extended (unfolded) chain built from real bond lengths and bond angles using a standard internal-coordinate placement algorithm. The helix ranges below come from the real, PDB-deposited secondary-structure assignment for this entry (helix-kink-helix, per the RCSB header for 1IYT) and are used only to bias the scoring function toward the known helical regions, not to preset the starting shape. This is a full backbone (N, C-alpha, C) torsion-space model, not the literal deposited atomic coordinates, and not an all-atom force field.
